short.headline: Nearly all Indians have electricity. So why are we still talking about energy access?
short.dek: Electricity access hit 99.5% in 2023, but cooking still relies on polluting fuels for one in four people, and per-person power use remains low.
short.body: In 2023, 99.5 percent of Indians lived in a household with an electricity connection, a remarkable jump from 50.9 percent in 1993, according to World Bank data. Yet access alone does not mean high consumption, the average Indian used just 1,181.63 kilowatt-hours of electricity that year, still a level that masks large disparities. And cooking tells a different story: only 76.7 percent had clean cooking fuels, leaving a significant share relying on wood, dung, or coal. Total energy use per person, at 25.84 million Btu in 2024, has grown but still includes a large share from traditional biomass. The page explains what the numbers mean and what they miss.

macha.heading: Current affairs, boss, what's the real story?
macha.body: We have a wire at home now, but the meter moves slowly, and the roti still cooks on wood smoke. That's the Indian energy story: a socket for almost everyone, but not everyone can afford to run a fridge or an induction stove. The data show we're past the first big hurdle, wiring the country, but still climbing the next two: getting affordable power to every plug and clean cooking into every kitchen. Total energy use per person is still low, which means we're not yet living a high-energy life like richer countries. So yes, the switch is on, but the bill is not small, for the household and for the country.
macha.soWhat: Ninety-nine percent access is a starting line, not a finish line.

article.title: Nearly all Indians have electricity. So why are we still talking about energy access?
article.standfirst: India achieved near-universal household electricity access by 2023, but cooking fuel and total energy use lag far behind. This data story examines the numbers behind the headlines.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## How many Indians now have access to electricity?

In 2023, 99.5 percent of India's population lived in a household with an electricity connection. That is up from 50.9 percent in 1993, according to the World Bank. This is the number that most directly answers the page question: yes, nearly all Indians now have a wire or off-grid system connecting their home to electricity.

But 'access' is a narrow definition. It counts a household as connected if it has a supply line, a solar home system, or a mini-grid connected to its dwelling. The indicator includes grid connections as well as off-grid solar kits, so even a small solar panel powering a few lights qualifies. It does not tell us whether the power flows for ten hours a day or twenty-four, whether the voltage is stable, or whether every room has a socket.

The line chart shows a steep climb after 2000, reflecting a national push that brought most of the population into the connected world. By 2023, the expansion had covered almost everyone. The remaining 0.5 percent represents those still without any connection, often in remote or difficult terrain.

The key caveat: having a connection is not the same as having reliable, adequate power. Many rural households experience load shedding, voltage drops, and diesel generator backup. The electrification headline is a milestone, not a finish line.

## Is cooking still a gap despite near‑universal electricity?

Yes. While 99.5 percent of Indians have electricity, only 76.7 percent had access to clean cooking fuels and technologies in 2023, the World Bank reports. That means about one in four Indians still rely primarily on burning wood, crop waste, dung, or coal for their daily meals.

Clean cooking access was just 22.7 percent in 2000, so the rise is significant, driven mainly by the expansion of LPG connections through the Ujjwala scheme and growing piped natural gas networks. But the gap to electricity access is wide. A household can have a metre and a fan but still cook over an open fire.

Why doesn't a wired home automatically switch to clean cooking? One visible reason in the data is that electricity bills and electric stoves remain expensive for many households. Another is that LPG cylinders need regular refilling, which can be unreliable or unaffordable. Cultural cooking preferences and the large pots used for traditional meals also favour biomass or LPG over small electric hotplates.

The line chart shows a steady, not spectacular, upward trend. At 76.7 percent, India has sharply expanded clean cooking access since 2000, but a significant share of the population still breathes smoke from solid fuels indoors. That smoke is linked to respiratory illness, and collecting fuel can consume hours of women's time each day.

Caveat: 'access' here means the primary cooking technology is clean. A household may use LPG for tea and still burn wood for the main meal. Practical daily use can be lower.

## How much electricity does the average Indian actually use?

Access says almost everyone has a connection. But how much do they consume? The average Indian used 1,181.63 kilowatt-hours of electricity in 2023, up from just 270.9 kWh in 1990. That is more than a fourfold rise, but the average still masks big disparities.

A kilowatt-hour is the energy needed to run a 1,000-watt appliance, like an iron or a microwave, for one hour. So 1,181.63 kWh per year works out to about 3.2 kWh per day per person. For a family of four, that's around 13 kWh daily, enough to power a few lights, a fan, a television, and maybe a refrigerator, but not much more.

The line chart shows a steady upward slope from 1990, accelerating after 2000 as connections grew and more of the economy electrified. But the average is pulled up by urban households, industries, and commercial users; a rural household might use a fraction of that amount.

Why does consumption rise more slowly than access? When a village first gets electricity, people often use it sparingly, for lighting and mobile charging, before slowly adding appliances as incomes rise. The grid reaching the home is step one; the step-up in usage can take a decade or more.

Caveat: this figure includes all electricity consumed in the country, factories, malls, railways, divided by the population. It is not a measure of what a typical household directly uses. And a rising average does not mean everyone's consumption is growing equally.

## What does total energy use per person reveal about energy poverty?

Electricity is only part of the energy story. Indians also need fuel for transport, industry, and cooking. The broadest measure is primary energy consumption per capita, all the coal, oil, gas, nuclear, hydro, solar, wind, and traditional biomass used in the country, divided by the population.

In 2024, India's energy consumption per person was 25.84 million British thermal units, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration. In 1980, it was just 4.99 million Btu. That is a fivefold increase over four decades.

A Btu is a tiny unit, about the heat from burning one wooden match. We use 'million Btu per person' to add together all forms of energy on one scale. In more familiar terms, 25.84 million Btu is roughly equivalent to 7,600 kilowatt-hours. This total includes losses from power plants and transmission, so it is larger than what end-users see.

The line chart shows a steady rise since 1980, with a notable acceleration after 2000. Yet a large share of this energy still comes from traditional biomass, wood, crop residue, dung, especially in rural homes. That biomass is often collected freely, so it does not always show up in commercial energy statistics but is estimated in the total.

Total energy per person is the clearest single number for measuring energy poverty. A rising number that still includes a large biomass share indicates that many people are not yet using much modern energy for lighting, transport, cooking, or productive work. India's number has grown, but the climb shows the energy transition is far from complete.

Caveat: the Btu unit is unfamiliar; one million Btu equals about 293 kilowatt-hours. And because the total includes biomass, a rising trend can mask a continued heavy dependence on traditional fuels, with rising use of fossil fuels layered on top.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: The data tracks electricity access, clean cooking access, electricity consumption per person, and total energy consumption per person for India over several decades. It shows a country that has almost achieved universal household electricity but still faces large gaps in clean cooking fuel and overall energy use, highlighting the difference between having a connection and genuinely escaping energy poverty.

pullQuotes.quote: The average Indian used 1,181.63 kWh of electricity in 2023, more than four times the 1990 level.
Only 76.7 percent of Indians had clean cooking fuel access in 2023, leaving about one in four still cooking on wood or dung.
pullQuotes.numberLabel: Access to clean fuels and technologies for cooking, latest

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Imagine your home has a switchboard and the electricity board has drawn a line to your house. That is 'access'. It counts you even if power comes only a few hours a day or the voltage is too low to run a motor. The indicator is the share of people living in a household with a connection, grid or off-grid. It does not measure reliability, quality, or how many appliances you can use.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It is the direct answer to whether Indians have electricity at home, but it is only the first rung on the energy ladder.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of the stove you use most. If it runs on LPG, electricity, biogas, or natural gas rather than firewood, crop waste, or dung cakes, you have clean cooking access. The World Bank measures the share of people whose primary cooking technology uses non-solid fuels. It does not guarantee that clean fuel is used for every meal or is always affordable.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It shows the next big energy challenge after lighting, cleaning up the kitchen smoke that affects health.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: A kilowatt-hour is the energy a 1,000-watt appliance (like a toaster or an iron) uses in one hour. It is the unit on your electricity bill. When we say per capita consumption is 1,181.63 kWh, we have added up all electricity used in the country, factories, malls, trains, and divided by the population. It is an average, not the amount a typical household uses.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It translates the abstract idea of electricity use into a concrete daily number and reveals that we are not yet a high-consumption country.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: A Btu is a tiny amount of heat, roughly the energy from burning one wooden match. We use 'million Btu per person' to add up different energy sources like coal, oil, and even firewood on one scale. 25.84 million Btu is like the energy in about 1,700 kg of firewood. This unit helps compare energy poverty across countries, but it is not something you see on a daily bill.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It lets us compare all energy types on one scale, but because it is unfamiliar, the page explains it with familiar comparisons.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: This is all primary energy used in India, for making electricity, running cars, cooking, industry, divided by the population. It includes the coal burned at power plants and the wood collected from forests. It is larger than what you and I directly use because it counts losses during power generation. A low number means a country is using little modern energy, which often means low mechanisation and high manual labor.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It is the widest lens on energy poverty and shows India still has a long way to go even though connections have surged.

dataSelectionAudit.areSelectedDataPointsGood: yes, the four indicators directly answer the question and provide essential context on the two biggest energy-access gaps (clean cooking and per-capita consumption)

visualizationDecisions.reason: A single long-run line with a few labelled points is the simplest way to show progress toward universal access on a phone screen.
The steady upward trend over 23 years is clearest in a line chart; it also lets readers compare the slope with electricity access.
A line chart shows the gradual climb and makes the gap between access (near 100%) and usage evident.
A line over four decades captures long-term energy poverty change without requiring the reader to understand sub-sources.

chartExplainers.title: Share of Indians with electricity
chartExplainers.takeaway: Electricity access soared from 50.9% in 1993 to 99.5% in 2023, reaching near-universal household connection.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart tracks the share of India's population living in a household with an electricity connection. The line climbs slowly in the 1990s, then rises steeply after 2000 as rural electrification schemes expanded. By 2023, it hits 99.5%, leaving only a small fraction of the population without any connection. The indicator counts grid connections, off-grid solar home systems, and mini-grids. It does not capture hours of supply, voltage quality, or whether a home has a meter and multiple rooms wired. Still, the sheer scale of progress is unmistakable: in three decades, India moved from half the country being in the dark to almost full coverage. The latest data point is an estimate based on survey and administrative data, so small year-to-year fluctuations should not be over-interpreted.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It directly answers the page question: do nearly all Indians have electricity? Yes.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Follow the line from 50.9% on the left in 1993 to 99.5% on the right in 2023; the steepest climb is between 2000 and 2015.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read this as 100% or assume that a connection means reliable, 24-hour supply.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a simple line with large labels, sparse tick marks, and no grid clutter.

chartExplainers.title: Access to clean cooking fuel
chartExplainers.takeaway: Only 76.7% of Indians had clean cooking fuel access in 2023, despite near-universal electricity, keeping nearly one in four in smoke-filled kitchens.
chartExplainers.detail: This line shows the percentage of the population primarily using non-solid fuels for cooking, LPG, electricity, biogas, or natural gas, since 2000. The line climbs from 22.7% to 76.7% by 2023, a 54-point rise over 23 years. That is impressive, but it lags far behind electricity access. The remaining share of the population still burns wood, crop residue, or dung. The trend steepens after 2016, likely driven by the Ujjwala LPG program, but the slope is still less dramatic than the electrification curve. This gap matters because indoor smoke from solid fuels leads to respiratory diseases and adds to the unpaid work of fuel collection. The gap between the two lines, electricity access and clean cooking access, is the most important visual contrast on the page.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It reveals the hidden gap behind the electrification headline: a wired home does not mean a clean kitchen.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Start at 22.7% in 2000 and trace the line up to 76.7% in 2023; mentally compare it with the electricity access line from the first chart at 99.5%.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume that 76.7% means clean cooking every meal; the survey captures the primary fuel, and stacking is common.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a line chart with a distinct dash pattern to separate it from the electricity access line when shown side by side.

chartExplainers.title: Electricity used per person
chartExplainers.takeaway: Per-person electricity consumption grew from 270.9 kWh in 1990 to 1,181.63 kWh in 2023, but remains very low relative to the access rate.
chartExplainers.detail: This line tracks total electricity consumed in India divided by the population. The number rose more than fourfold in 33 years, but at 1,181.63 kWh per year, the average Indian uses roughly 3.2 kWh per day. That is the amount of electricity a small window air conditioner eats in three hours. The line rises steadily, with a pause only during the 2020 pandemic dip, before resuming. The growth reflects rising incomes, expansion of industries, and more appliances in homes. Yet the average masks inequality: a small fraction of urban and industrial users consume a large share, while many rural households use only a few units a day. The steady slope shows that consumption growth has not matched the vertical spike in access, wiring a home is cheaper and faster than filling it with fans, TV sets, and fridges.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It shows that access does not automatically translate into high usage, the real measure of modern energy living.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the line rising from 270.9 kWh in 1990 to 1,181.63 kWh in 2023; the 2023 value is about 4.4 times the 1990 level.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not multiply the per-capita number by your household size and assume that is what you use; it is a national average, not a typical home's bill.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A clean line chart with a large final value label and a few reference years marked works best on a small screen.

chartExplainers.title: Total energy used per person
chartExplainers.takeaway: Total energy per person jumped from 4.99 million Btu in 1980 to 25.84 million Btu in 2024, but a large share still comes from traditional biomass.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart adds up all forms of primary energy, coal, oil, natural gas, nuclear, hydro, renewables, and biomass, and shows the average per Indian. In 2024, it stood at 25.84 million Btu, roughly five times the 1980 level of 4.99 million Btu. To make sense of a Btu, remember one matchstick gives roughly one Btu; 25.84 million matches worth of heat per person per year. That energy powers not only electricity but also transport, industry, and cooking. The steepest rise follows the year 2000, mirroring India's economic acceleration. However, a chunk of this total still comes from traditional biomass, which is often collected for free and burned inefficiently. So a rising line does not guarantee a shift to modern, clean fuels. The composition of the energy mix is as important as the absolute level.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It provides the fullest measure of energy poverty, revealing that even with electrification, overall energy use per person remains closely tied to traditional sources.
chartExplainers.howToRead: See the line climb from 4.99 in 1980 to 25.84 in 2024; the unit is a heat unit, so think of it as a stack of 25.84 million wooden matches per person.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not compare Btu directly with kWh; 1 million Btu ≈ 293 kWh. Also, the total includes biomass, so a rise does not exclusively mean more fossil fuel or electricity use.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: An uncluttered line with a single y-axis and a few decade markers can make the unfamiliar unit accessible.

sourceNotes: World Bank, 'Access to electricity (% of population)'.
World Bank, 'Access to clean fuels and technologies for cooking (% of population)'.
World Bank, 'Electric power consumption (kWh per capita)'.
U.S. Energy Information Administration, 'Energy consumption per capita (million Btu per person)'.
caveats: Electricity access does not measure reliability or quality of supply.
Clean cooking access counts primary cooking technology, not daily practice.
Electricity consumption per capita is a national average that masks wide rural-urban and income gaps.
Total energy per capita includes traditional biomass estimates with high uncertainty.
The Btu unit is unfamiliar to most Indian readers; equivalent comparisons are provided in the text.
All data are national aggregates and may not reflect interstate variations.

editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: Nearly all Indians now have electricity access, 99.5% in 2023, but access alone does not guarantee high usage or clean cooking.
requiredConcepts: Define electricity access as the percentage of the population with a household electricity connection, distinguishing it from reliability or consumption.
Explain clean cooking access as the share of the population using non-solid fuels (LPG, electricity, biogas, etc.) and why a wired home may still burn wood.
Define kWh per capita as total electricity consumed by the country divided by population, showing average use rather than access.
Define million Btu per person as a measure of total primary energy consumption per capita, covering all sources (coal, oil, gas, biomass, etc.).
State the most important caveat: the 99.5% access figure masks gaps in cooking fuel, low per capita consumption, and quality of supply.

sectionPlan.heading: How many Indians now have access to electricity?
sectionPlan.job: Show the rise from 50.9% in 1993 to 99.5% in 2023, explain what 'access' means, and frame it as the direct answer to the page question.
conceptsToExplain: The difference between having a connection and reliable supply
sectionPlan.caveat: Access does not measure how many hours a day power is available or whether it reaches all parts of a household.

sectionPlan.heading: Is cooking still a gap despite near‑universal electricity?
sectionPlan.job: Contrast clean cooking access (76.7%) with electricity access, explain the health and time burden of traditional fuels, and show the steady rise from 22.7% in 2000.
lockedNumbersToUse: Access to clean fuels and technologies for cooking, latest
Access to clean fuels and technologies for cooking, earliest
conceptsToExplain: Why a household with electricity might still use biomass for cooking (cost, tradition, reliability)
sectionPlan.caveat: The definition includes fuels that may not be consistently affordable or available; practical daily use can be lower.

sectionPlan.heading: How much electricity does the average Indian actually use?
sectionPlan.job: Show that per‑person electricity consumption rose from 270.9 kWh to 1,181.63 kWh, but clarify that access does not mean high usage, and note the wide gap between average and intensive use.
conceptsToExplain: Per capita consumption as an average
Why growth in access and growth in consumption happen at different paces
sectionPlan.caveat: This is an all‑India average; rural households and poorer states consume far less.

sectionPlan.heading: What does total energy use per person reveal about energy poverty?
sectionPlan.job: Broaden the frame to all energy (coal, oil, gas, biomass, etc.) and show the jump from 4.99 to 25.84 million Btu per person, while explaining why Btu is used and what the number implies for quality of life.
conceptsToExplain: Why total energy per person is a better poverty lens than electricity alone
sectionPlan.caveat: Btu is unfamiliar to most Indian readers; the absolute level is still low by global standards, and a large share may still come from traditional biomass.

chartPlan.title: Share of Indians with electricity
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The direct answer: how close India is to universal household electricity access.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line starts at 50.9% in 1993 and rises steeply after 2000, ending at 99.5% in 2023. Each point is the share of the population living in a household with an electricity connection.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read the latest value as 100% or assume that a connection means reliable, 24‑hour supply.

chartPlan.title: Access to clean cooking fuel
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The gap the headline hides: a wired home can still cook on wood or dung.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line rises from 22.7% in 2000 to 76.7% in 2023. The slope is steady, not sudden, and the end point is well below the electricity access line.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume that ‘clean cooking access’ means a household uses the fuel every day; it only means the primary cooking technology is clean.

chartPlan.title: Electricity used per person
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Access is not the same as use: per‑person electricity is still low by world standards.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line climbs from 270.9 kWh in 1990 to 1,181.63 kWh in 2023. This is total electricity consumed divided by population, not the amount each person directly uses.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not treat the average as what a typical household consumes; industrial and commercial use raises the average without raising household consumption equally.

chartPlan.title: Total energy used per person
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The widest frame: all energy per person, the clearest single read on energy poverty.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line goes from 4.99 million Btu in 1980 to 25.84 million Btu in 2024. It includes every source, coal, oil, gas, nuclear, renewables, and traditional biomass.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not compare Btu numbers directly with kWh; 1 million Btu is roughly 293 kWh, so the unit is much larger. Also, a rising line can still hide heavy reliance on traditional biomass.

dataSelectionAudit.areSelectedDataPointsGood: yes, the four indicators directly answer the question and provide essential context on the two biggest energy‑access gaps (clean cooking and per‑capita consumption)

visualizationDecisions.reason: A single long‑run line with a few labelled points is the simplest way to show progress toward universal access on a phone screen.
The steady upward trend over 23 years is clearest in a line chart; it also lets readers compare the slope with electricity access.
A line chart shows the gradual climb and makes the gap between access (near 100%) and usage evident.
A line over four decades captures long‑term energy poverty change without requiring the reader to understand sub‑sources.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The share of people living in a household that has an electricity connection, regardless of how much or how reliably they use it.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It is the headline number that answers the page question, but it is a starting point, not the full picture.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The share of people whose primary cooking technology uses non‑solid fuels (LPG, electricity, biogas, natural gas), avoiding smoke from wood, crop waste, or dung.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It shows that nearly a quarter of Indians still rely on polluting fuels, even if they have an electricity connection.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A unit of electrical energy. One kWh is the energy needed to run a 1,000‑watt appliance for one hour. Per‑capita figures divide total national consumption by population.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: kWh per capita is the standard measure for comparing how much electricity people actually use, not just whether they have a socket.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A unit of heat energy. One Btu is roughly the heat from burning one wooden match. It is used to add up all forms of energy, coal, oil, gas, biomass, on a single scale.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Total energy per person in Btu captures the full energy picture, but the unit is unfamiliar; we translate it into relatable comparisons.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: All primary energy used in a country (for electricity, transport, industry, cooking, etc.) divided by population. It includes losses in generation and transmission, so it is larger than what end‑users see.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It is the broadest measure of energy poverty; a low number means people are using little modern energy even if some indicators look good.

proseRules: No outside facts beyond the evidence packet.
No calculations unless the evidence packet supplies the derived number.
Question‑led H2 sections that genuinely answer the heading, not merely describe chart shapes.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
